State of California
Fair Political Practices Commission
P.O. BOX 807 • SACRAMENTO, 95804 • • • 1100 K STREET BUILDING, SACRAMENTO, 95814

Technical Assistance • • Administration • • Executive/Legal • • Enforcement • • Statements of Economic Intere
(916) 322-5662            322-5660              322-5901            322-6441            322-6444

November 5, 1982


John M. Cohan
Deputy County Counsel
105 East Anapamu St.
Santa Barbara, CA  93101-2063

                                        Re:  Advice Request No. A-82-155

Dear Mr. Cohan:

     Thank you for your request for advice on behalf of Santa
Barbara County Planning Commissioners Margaret S. Hamister,
Donald E. Lahr and Kenneth R. Stillman.

                                    FACTS

     The facts, as I understand them, are that Santa Barbara
County is currently reviewing and revising its zoning
ordinance.  This review includes that portion of the zoning
ordinance regulating land use for oil and gas facilities
(Divisions 8 and 10).  The County's Resource Management
Department began the review process by preparing a draft of
Divisions 8 and 10, containing the staff's recommendations.  This
draft was presented to the Planning Commission and hearings were
conducted.  As a result of these hearings, and after discussions
by the Planning Commission, a final Revised Hearing Draft of
Divisions 8 and 10 (dated 8/19/82) was prepared, containing
textual changes requested by the Commission.

     The Planning Commission will vote on whether or not to
approve the Revised Hearing Draft.  If passed, the Draft will be
submitted to the Board of Supervisors where it may be adopted,
modified or disapproved.  (See Government Code Sections
65854-65857)1/  However, the recommendations of the Planning

___________________
1/  Hereinafter all statutory references are to the
Government Code, unless otherwise indicated.

John M. Cohan
November 5, 1982
Page 2


Commission will greatly influence the Board of Supervisors in
its final decision regarding the oil and gas regulations.

     The changes proposed by the Planning Commission would alter
the existing oil and gas zoning provisions and permit procedures.  These changes include:

     1.  Banning oil development in seven areas where
development was previously allowed;

     2.  Streamlining the permit procedure for oil and gas
exploration and production drilling by eliminating a
requirement that oil companies obtain both a rezoning and a
permit, and requiring, instead, that only a Conditional Use
Permit be obtained;

     3.  Establishing more stringent "development
standards"2/ that must be met for exploratory oil and gas
drilling; onshore oil and gas production; treatment and
processing facilities; refining; marine terminals; and oil
and gas pipelines;

     4.  Establishing a procedure whereby a ministerially
granted exploration and production permit may be obtained if
a project meets certain, established criteria.1/ But if
the criteria are not met, a discretionary permit must be
sought from the Planning Commission.  This new procedure
provides the Planning Commission with more discretion than
under the existing system.4/

_______________________

     2/ For example, in the Revised Hearing Draft, the
"development standards" for treatment and processing facilities
require noise and noxious odor limitations, a fence surrounding
the facility and provisions to prevent erosion and flood damage.

     3/  These criteria establish that the project will not
have a significant environmental impact.

     4/ Under the existing system, a discretionary, rather
than ministerial, permit must be obtained if drilling will be
within 1,000 feet of a zone on which drilling is not allowed.
The new system requires a discretionary permit if drilling is
within this 1,000 foot setback, as well as in many other
situations, such as where the company plans to build a treatment
or processing facility.

John M. Cohan
November 5, 1982
Page 3


     5. Allowing oil companies additional flexibility in
obtaining permits for exploratory wells. Currently, one
permit is issued for both the exploration and production
stages of oil and gas development. In the case of
unproductive wells,5/ this results in unnecessary, lengthy
environmental review of the production phase. The proposed
procedures allow oil companies to first file for an
exploration permit and later file for a production permit,
if the well is successful.

     6. Replacing existing, vague provisions for oil and
gas pipelines with provisions requiring the Planning
Commission to issue discretionary permits. "Development
standards" are also established for pipelines (see #3,
above).

     Your question is whether Commissioners Hamister, Lahr and
Stillman vote on the Revised Hearing Draft, or whether they
have conflicts of interests requiring them to abstain.

PLANNING COMMISSIONER HAMISTER

     Planning Commissioner Hamister currently owns common stock
in Exxon Corporation, valued at between $10,000 and $100,000.
Section 87100 provides that no local government official shall
make, or participate in the making of, a governmental decision
in which she knows, or has reason to know, she has a financial
interest. An official has a financial interest in a decision
within the meaning of Section 87100 if it is reasonably
foreseeable that the decision will have a material financial
effect on any business entity in which the public official has a
direct or indirect investment worth more than $1,000. (Section
87103(a)). Thus, Commissioner Hamister must abstain from making
and participating in any decisions which will materially affect
Exxon Corporation.

     In voting to approve or reject the final draft of the
proposed changes to the zoning ordinance, Commissioner Hamister
will be making and participating in the making of a governmental

_______________________

     5/ Most of the exploratory drilling results in
unproductive wells.

John M. Cohan
November 5, 1982
Page 4


decision.6/ It is true that the Board of Supervisors makes
the final decision to change the oil and gas regulations,
however, the law specifies that the Planning Commission has a
major role in any zoning ordinance change. The Commission is
required to hold hearings and to make a written recommendation
(the Final Revised Hearing Draft) to the Board of Supervisors
and, if the Board deviates from the recommendations, the Board
must ask for an additional report and recommendation from the
Planning Commission (Sections 65854-65857). Thus, the content
of the Final Revised Hearing Draft will substantially influence
the final changes that will be made to the oil and gas
regulations.

A decision to approve or reject the draft of the proposed
zoning changes will have a material financial effect on a
company the size of Exxon Corporation, if it is reasonably
foreseeable that the proposed changes would increase or decrease
Exxon's annualized gross income by $100,000, its annual net
income by $50,000, or the value of its assets or liabilities by
$100,000. (2 Cal. Adm. Code Section 18702(b)(1)(A)-(C).)1/
In determining whether such a financial effect is foreseeable,
certainty is not required and there must only be a "substantial
likelihood" that the effect will occur. (Thorner Opinion, No.
75-089, 1 FPPC Opinions 198, copy enclosed.)

It is reasonably foreseeable that the proposed changes will
have a material financial effect on Exxon Corporation, if the

6/ 2 Cal. Adm. Code Section 18700(b)(1) states that a
public official "makes a governmental decision" when, acting
within the authority of his or her office, he or she votes on a
matter.

2 Cal. Adm. Code Section 18700(c)(2)(B) provides that a
public employee "participates in the making of a governmental
decision when, acting within the authority of her position, he
or she advises or makes recommendations to the decision-maker,
either directly or without significant intervening substantive
review, by preparing or presenting any report, analysis or
opinion, orally or in writing, which requires the exercise of
judgment on the part of the official and the purpose of which is
to influence the decision.

7/ 2 Cal. Adm. Code Section 18702(b)(1)(A)-(C) provides
that, in determining whether a decision's effect on a business
entity will be "material," consideration should be given to
whether the effect of the decision will be to increase or
decrease:

John M. Cohan
November 5, 1982
Page 5


company plans to continue the exploration, drilling and
construction of oil and gas facilities in the area. The
proposed changes will, among other things, affect the company by
restricting the amount of developable land, altering the permit
procedure, and subjecting its permit requests to additional
discretionary review. Thus, Commissioner Hamister is required
to disqualify herself from voting to approve or reject the Final
Revised Hearing Draft.

PLANNING COMMISSIONER LAHR

Planning Commissioner Lahr is the president and major
shareholder (86%)8/ of Lahr Electric Motors, which repairs and
sells electric motors to oil companies.9/ His ownership
interest is worth more than $100,000l0/ and thus, he is
prohibited from making and participating in any decision, which

(Footnote 7 continued)
(A) The annualized gross revenues by the lesser of:

1. $100,000; or

2. One percent if the effect is $1,000 or more;
or

(B) Annual net income by the lesser of:

1. $50,000; or

2. One half of one percent if the effect if
$1,000 or more; or

(C) Current assets or liabilities by the lesser of:

1. $100,000; or

2. One half of one percent if the effect is
$1,000 or more.

8/ For purposes of the Political Reform Act, an
official's ownership interest in a business entity includes any
interest owned directly, indirectly or beneficially by his or
her immediate family. (Section 82034.)

9/ Among other clients.

10/ This was disclosed on his 1981 Statement of Economic
Interests.

John M. Cohan
November 5, 1982
Page 6


will have a reasonably foreseeable material financial effect on
the company. (Sections 87100 and 87103(a).)11/

A decision will have a material financial effect on a
company the size of Lahr Motors,12/ if it is reasonably
foreseeable that the decision will increase or decrease the
company's annualized gross revenues by 1 percent, its net income
by 1/2 percent, or the value of its assets or liabilities by
1/2 percent. The gross income of Lahr Motors, in 1981, was
$600,000. Thus, if it is reasonably foreseeable that the
proposed zoning and permit changes, if adopted, will increase or
decrease the company's gross revenues by $6,000, or meet any of
the other percentage tests, then Commissioner Lahr must
disqualify himself.

It is reasonably foreseeable that the proposed zoning
changes will restrict oil and gas drilling and, therefore, cause
a potential decline in the company's motor sales. However, Lahr
Motors sells motors to businesses other than oil companies and
it performs repairs, neither of which will be affected by the
proposed zoning changes. Commissioner Lahr must, in good faith,
determine whether it is reasonably foreseeable that the proposed
changes, if implemented, will affect his business to the extent
described in the percentage tests. He must disqualify himself
from the vote, if necessary.

Because Commissioner Lahr owns 86.1% of Lahr Motors, income
to the company is treated as income to Lahr on a pro rata basis
(Section 82030). Therefore, Commissioner Lahr is also
prohibited from making and participating in a decision which
will have a reasonably foreseeable material financial effect on
any clients of Lahr Motors who provided the company with
sufficient gross income, during the 12 months prior to the vote
on the Revised Hearing Draft,13/ that Commission Lahr's pro
rata share of the income (86%) was $250 or more. (To meet this

     11/ He is also prohibited from making and participating
in decisions that will have a reasonably foreseeable material
financial effect on Lahr Motors because he is the president of
the company and because it is a source of income to him of $250
or more. (See Sections 87100 and 87103(c) and (d).)

     12/ The test for a "material" financial effect varies
with the amount of a company's annualized gross income, net
income, and assets and liabilities.

     13/ Assuming that the Planning Commission will vote on
the changes in approximately November 1982, the period covered
begins in November 1981.

John M. Cohan
November 5, 1982
Page 7


threshold a client only needed to pay $291 to Lahr Motors.) Many
of the clients who paid Lahr Motors $291 or more are oil
companies who are actively developing and producing oil and gas
in Santa Barbara County. If it is reasonably foreseeable that
any of these companies will be materially affected by the
proposed zoning and permit changes, then Commissioner Lahr must
disqualify himself from making or participating in the vote. A
company must have fairly limited activities in the area not to
be materially affected by the potential wide-spread impact of
the proposed zoning and permit changes. However, the facts of
each case must be applied to the guidelines established in
2 Cal. Adm. Code Section 18702(b)(3)(C).14/

COMMISSIONER STILLMAN

Commissioner Stillman owns a little over 1 percent of the
stock issued by Lompoc Oil Developing Company, worth over
$1,000, and he sits on the company's Board of Directors. Lompoc
Oil owns the mineral rights to approximately 750 acres in Santa
Barbara County.15/ The mineral rights on 550 of the acres are
leased to Santa Fe Energy Company, which has not begun drilling
on the land.16/ Lompoc Oil retained the mineral rights to the
remaining 200 acres, but has not yet developed them.

Because Commissioner Stillman has an ownership interest in
Lompoc Oil, valued at over $1,000, and, because he is a director
of the company, he may not vote to approve or reject the

14/ See the discussion regarding Exxon Corporation
contained in this letter.

Union Oil Company was a client of Lahr Motors (although it
is unclear how much Union Oil paid the company between November
1981 and November 1982) and it does extensive work in Santa
Barbara County. If the amount paid during the 12 month period
was $291 or more, then Commissioner Lahr must disqualify
himself.

15/ The facts regarding Lompoc Oil are drawn from your
letters; my conversations with your office; a letter from
David A. Anderson, attorney for Get Oil Out, Inc., and GOO Legal
and Educational Fund, Inc.; and an article from the Santa
Barbara News-Press dated May 27, 1982.

16/ If any future drilling by Santa Fe Energy Company is
successful, Lompoc Oil will receive a portion of the profit.

John M. Cohan
November 5, 1982
Page 8


Proposed zoning and permit changes, if the changes, if adopted,
will have a reasonably forseeable material financial effect on
Lompoc Oil. (Sections 87100 and 87103(a) and (d).) A decision
will have a material financial effect on a company the size of
Lompoc Oil (which had $7,250 in gross income and $5,000 in net
income for 1981) if the decision will affect the company by
$1,000 or more. (2 Cal. Adm. Code Section 18700(b)(1)(A)-(C).)

The mineral rights owned by Lompoc Oil are on land zoned
Agricultural - 100 (meaning that there is a 100 acre minimum).
Under the existing zoning ordinance, drilling is allowed on
this type of land as a matter of right, unless the drilling is
within 1,000 feet of another zone that does not allow drilling.
In the latter case, a Conditional Use Permit must be obtained,
which involves a discretionary review by the Planning
Commission. Under the proposed zoning changes, the 700 acres
will probably be designated as Agricultural II land. On
Agricultural II land, drilling is allowed as a matter of right,
if the land meets certain criteria. If the criteria are not
met, an Exploratory Plan must be prepared and the Planning
Commission's discretionary approval must be obtained.

Based on these facts, it is not reasonably foreseeable that
the proposed changes will have a material financial effect on
Lompoc Oil because there will be little or no impact on the
company's gross income, net income, or assets. The changes may,
ultimately, cost Lompoc Oil some additional money in permit
costs, however, the company currently has no plans to develop
the 200 acres and the remaining 550 acres will be developed by
Santa Fe Energy Company. Also, any increase in the Planning
Commission's discretion in granting or denying permits is
unlikely to affect the company because, assuming that a
discretionary permit is necessary at all, the zoning of the land
makes the approval of a permit highly probable.17/ Thus,
Commissioner Stillman may vote on the proposed changes, as he
does not appear to have a conflict.

CONCLUSION

If Exxon Corporation plans to continue to explore, drill and
develop oil and gas facilities in Santa Barbara County, then it

_____________________

17/ Land zoned Agricultural II is one of three types of
land deemed most conducive to oil and gas drilling and
development. Also, the land surrounding this property has
already been approved for such activities.

John M. Cohan
November 5, 1982
Page 9


is reasonably foreseeable that the company will be materially
affected by the proposed changes and Commissioner Hamister must
disqualify herself from voting. Also, Commissioner Lahr must
make a good faith determination as to whether he must disqualify
himself from the vote. It appears likely that the proposed
changes, if adopted, will either materially affect clients of
his business who are sources of income to him, or the business
itself. In either case, disqualification is necessary. Lastly,
Commissioner Stillman does not have a conflict of interest and
may vote on the proposed changes.

I would like to point out that the provisions of the
Political Reform Act only allow this office to render advice as
to whether a future action of a public official will constitute
a violation of the act. Consequently, I cannot provide you with
advice on whether Commissioners Hamister and Lahr18/ violated
the Act, in the past, by making or participating in the making
of decisions involving changes to the oil and gas regulations.
In addition, the staff's advice provides the Commissioners with
immunity from enforcement actions19/ only if the advice is
requested prior to the violation20/ (2 Cal. Adm. Code Section
18329).

You asked about the effect of the Commission's past
activities on the decisions which have already been made. Even
if the past activities were in violation of the Act, the
provisions of the Political Reform Act do not invalidate these
decisions. However, under special circumstances, these
decisions may be set aside in a lawsuit brought by a person
residing in the jurisdiction. (Section 91003(b).)


18/ Assuming that Commissioner Lahr determines that he
has a conflict of interest.

19/ Enforcement actions brought by the Fair Political
Practices Commission.

20/ The issue of possible past violations must be
addressed by our Enforcement Division or the Santa Barbara
County District Attorney's Office.

John M. Cohan
November 5, 1982
Page 10


     I hope my letter is helpful to you.  If you have any
additional questions, please feel free to contact me at
(916) 322-5901.

                                        Very truly yours,


                                        [signature]
                                        Janis Shank McLean
                                        Counsel
                                        Legal Division

JSM:plh

KENNETH L. NELSON
County Counsel

MARVIN LEVINE
Chief Assistant

COUNTY COUNSEL
SANTA BARBARA COUNTY

105 East Anapamu Street
Santa Barbara, CA 93101-2063

Telephone: (805) 963-7189

September 7, 1982


Fair Political Practices
Commission
P.O. Box 807
1100 K Street Building
Sacramento, CA 95804

RE: Opinion Request --
Santa Barbara County Planning Commission

We request that the Commission advise us as to whether the
following three Planning Commissioners may participate in the
hearings and decision of the Planning Commission on revisions
to the County's Planning and Zoning Ordinance as they pertain
to oil and gas energy development.

The Planning Commission is revising the Planning and Zoning
Ordinance pursuant to its authority under Gov. Code § 65853
et seq. Especially see § 65855. As part of the proposed
Zoning Ordinance Divisions 8 and 10 deal with matters
affecting the oil and gas industry. A summary of Division 8
is attached as Exhibit A. Attached as Exhibits B and C
respectively are copies of Division 8 "Energy Facilities,"
in its original and in its most recently revised forms.
Attached as Exhibit D is a copy of Division 10, "Permit
Procedures."

Our request for this advice arises from a citizen's complaint
of a possible conflict as to Commissioners Lahr, Hamister and
Stillman. Copies of their disclosure statements are attached
as Exhibits E, F and G.

Some additional factual data may be helpful.

COMMISSIONER LAHR

Commissioner Lahr lists Lahr Electrical Motors, Inc. as both
an investment and an income source. The company deals in the

Fair Political Practices
Commission                                2                           9/7/82


repair and sales of electrical motors.  Listed as clients which
contribute greater than $10,000.00 a year are three oil
companies: Getty Oil, Mobil Oil and Union Oil.

Commissioner Lahr also has advised me of these facts:

Getty Oil Company -- Annual gross business in 1981 was $45,000,
all of which was for work within Santa Barbara County.  His
company competes with two others for this work.

Mobil Oil Company -- 1981 gross receipts were $35,460.00.  All
business performed was outside the County of Santa Barbara (in
Monterey County).

Union Oil Company -- 1981 gross receipts were $7,130.00 of which
approximately one-half of the work was done in San Luis Obispo
County.

Commissioner Lahr advises the company's approximate gross
revenue was $600,000.00 in 1981.

COMMISSIONER HAMISTER

Commissioner Hamister holds a one-half interest as community
property in 500 shares of Exxon stock.  She knows of no other
interests or holdings in oil and gas related companies.

COMMISSIONER STILLMAN

Commissioner Stillman inherited 2,700 shares of stock in Lompoc
Oil and Development.  This company was formed in the early part
of this century by a subscription sale of approximately 250,000
shares of stock.  At one time the company owned approximately
700 acres in Sabata Canyon in the County.  Active oil explora-
tion was conducted until about 1945 when the last of three test
wells revealed no economic oil.  Several years ago the company
sold the land itself but retained the mineral rights.  The land
is now being developed for agricultural use.  The mineral rights
have been leased to an oil company.  Commissioner Stillman does
not know the name of the oil company.  He does not know what, if
any, exploration is being conducted by the oil company.

AFFECT OF THE PROPOSED ORDINANCE

We have been unable to reach any conclusions about the nature
of any financial impact of the proposed ordinance upon the
oil and gas industry.  However, it has been the position of the

Fair Political Practices
Commission                           3                              9/7/82


Resource Management Department staff who drafted the proposed
Energy Facilities Division that the impact upon the oil and
gas industry in terms of financial or administrative burden
will be minor.

However, it should be noted the oil and gas representatives
have contested this conclusion at workshops and public
hearings.  The workshops involved sessions in which Resource
Management staff and industry representatives met to attempt
to resolve disputes over proposed sections of the ordinance.
The public hearings were all held before the Planning Commission.
A copy of the Western Oil and Gas Association's letter of
May 13, 1982 with its proposed ordinance is attached as Exhibit H.

A copy of the Resource Management Department's staff report of
March 19, 1982 on the oil industries recommended changes is
attached at Exhibit I.

The public hearings on the Energy Facilities Division are still
ongoing.  The next is scheduled to be conducted today.  It is
my intention to advise the Commission to defer any further
consideration of Divisions 8 and 10 as they pertain to the oil
and gas industry until we have received an opinion from your
office.  We will of course be happy to provide you with any
additional information you feel would be helpful in reaching
your decision.

                                        Kenneth L. Nelson
                                        County Counsel

                                   By [illegible]
                                        John M. Cohan
                                        Deputy County Counsel
JMC:L

cc:  Planning Commissioners

State of California
Fair Political Practices Commission
P.O. BOX 807 • SACRAMENTO, 95804 • • • 1100 K STREET BUILDING, SACRAMENTO, 95814

Technical Assistance • • Administration • • Executive/Legal • • Statements of Economic Interest
(916) 322-5662          322-5660          322-5901          322-6441          322-6444


November 22, 1982


Stanley M. Roden
Santa Barbara County
District Attorney's Office
118 East Figueroa Street
Santa Barbara, CA  93103

Re:  Our Advice Letter No. A-82-155

Dear Mr. Roden:

     Our office recently responded to a request for advice from
Mr. Jack Cohan of the Santa Barbara County Counsel's Office.
(See the enclosed copy of our response.)  His request was made
on behalf of County Planning Commissioners Margaret Hamister,
Donald Lahr and Kenneth Stillman.  Mr. Cohan asked us to
determine whether these Commissioners had conflicts of
interests under the Political Reform Act, requiring them to
abstain from making and participating in the making of decisions
concerning changes to the oil and gas land use regulations
contained in the County's Zoning Ordinance.  This letter is to
advise you of the facts of this case, as they suggest that
Planning Commissioners Hamister and Lahr may have violated the
Political Reform Act.

FACTS

     Santa Barbara County is currently reviewing and revising its
Zoning Ordinance.  This review includes that portion of the
zoning ordinance regulating land use for oil and gas facilities
(Divisions 8 and 10).  The County's Resource Management
Department began the review process by preparing a draft of
Divisions 8 and 10, containing the staff's recommendations.
This draft was presented to the Planning Commission, who held
hearings, requested textual changes, and ultimately developed the
Final Revised Hearing Draft.  The changes proposed by the
Planning Commission would substantially alter the existing oil

Stanley M. Roden
November 22, 1982
Page 2


and gas zoning provisions and permit procedures.  (see my advice
letter for a summary of these changes.)

     The Planning Commission will vote to approve or reject the
Revised Hearing Draft.  If passed, the Draft will be submitted
to the Board of Supervisors where it may be adopted, modified or
disapproved.  (See Government Code Sections 65854-65857.)1/
However, the recommendations of the Planning Commission will
greatly influence the Board of Supervisors in its final decision
regarding the oil and gas regulations.

     Although the Planning Commission has not yet voted on the
proposed changes, there has been preliminary work and
discussion.  The Political Reform Act prevents public officials
from making, or participating in the making of, governmental
decisions (Section 87100).  An official "participates in the
making of a governmental decision" when, acting within the
authority of his or her position, he or she:

                    . . .

     (2)  Advises or makes recommendations to the
decision-maker, either directly or without significant
intervening substantive review, by:

          (A)  Conducting research or making any
investigation which requires the exercise of
judgment on the part of the official . . . and the
purpose of which is to influence the decision; or

          (B)  Preparing or presenting any report,
analysis or opinion, orally or in writing, which
requires the exercise of judgment on the part of
the official and the purpose of which is to
influence the decision.

               (2 Cal. Adm. Code Section
               18700(c)(2)(A) and (B).)

     The following discussion considers the application of the
provisions of the Political Reform Act to the facts surrounding
the financial interests of each of the Commissioners and
indicates where violations of the Act may have occurred.

_________________
     1/  Hereinafter all statutory references are to the
Government Code, unless otherwise indicated.

Stanley M. Roden
November 22, 1982
Page 3


PLANNING COMMISSIONER HAMISTER

Planning Commissioner Hamister currently owns common stock
in Exxon Corporation, valued at between $10,001 and $100,000.
Section 87100 provides that no local government official shall
make, or participate in the making of, a governmental decision
in which she knows, or has reason to know, she has a financial
interest. An official has a financial interest in a decision
within the meaning of section 87100 if it is reasonably
foreseeable that the decision will have a material financial
effect on any business entity in which the public official has a
direct or indirect investment worth more than $1,000. (Section
87103(a)). Thus, Commissioner Hamister must abstain from making
and participating in any decisions which will materially affect
Exxon Corporation. Under Commission regulations, a decision
will have a material financial effect on a company the size of
Exxon, if it is reasonably foreseeable that the effect of the
decision will be to increase or decrease Exxon's annualized
gross income by $100,000, its annual net income by $50,000, or
the value of its assets or liabilities by $100,000. (2 Cal.
Adm. Code Section 18702(b)(1)(A)-(C).)

As I discussed in my advice letter to Mr. Cohan, it is
reasonably foreseeable that the decision to amend the oil and
gas regulations is a decision will have a material financial
effect on Exxon Corporation. (See my letter for the analysis.)
Thus, Commissioner Hamister is and was prohibited from making
or participating in making of, this decision.

In my advice letter, I confined my analysis to whether it
was proper for Commissioner Hamister to vote on the future
final decision to approve or reject the Final Revised Draft of
the changes to the oil and gas regulations. However,
Commissioner Hamister may have already participated in
discussions or decisions occurring prior to this final vote. In
my letter, I did not discuss the propriety of her participation
and any enforcement action would, of course, focus on these past
activities.

PLANNING COMMISSIONER LAHR

Planning Commissioner Lahr is the president and major
shareholder (86%)2/ of Lahr Electric Motors, which repairs and

_______________________

2/ For purposes of the Political Reform Act, an
official's ownership interest in a business entity includes any
interest owned directly, indirectly or beneficially by his or
her immediate family. (Section 82034.)

Stanley M. Roden
November 22, 1982
Page 4


sells electric motors to oil companies.3/ His ownership
interest is worth more than $100,0004/ and thus, he is
prohibited from making and participating in any decision, in
which it is reasonably foreseeable that it will have a material
financial effect on the company. (Sections 87100 and
87103(a).)5/

A decision has a material financial effect on a company the
size of Lahr Motors, if it increases or decreases the company's
annualized gross revenues by 1 percent, its net income by
1/2 percent, or the value of its assets or liabilities by
1/2 percent. The gross income of Lahr Motors, in 1981, was
$600,000. Thus, if it is reasonably foreseeable that the
proposed zoning and permit changes, if adopted, will increase or
decrease the company's gross revenues by $6,000, or meet any of
the other percentage tests, then Commissioner Lahr must
disqualify himself from the future final vote and he should have
abstained from any past participation in this decision.

It is reasonably foreseeable that the proposed zoning
changes will restrict oil and gas drilling. This change has the
potential to cause a decline in the company's motor sales.
However, Lahr Motors sells motors to and performs repairs for
other businesses than oil companies. Thus, much of its business
will not be affected by the proposed zoning changes. In my
letter, I advised Commissioner Lahr that he must, in good faith,
determine whether it is reasonably foreseeable that the proposed
changes, if implemented, will affect his business to the extent
described in the percentage tests.

Because Commissioner Lahr owns 86% of Lahr Motors, a
pro rata (86%) share of the income to the company is treated as
income to Lahr (Section 82030). Therefore, any client who paid
Lahr Motors at least $291 or more in the past 12 months

_______________________

3/ Among other clients.

4/ This was disclosed on his 1981 Statement of Economic
Interests.

5/ He is also prohibited from making and participating in
decisions that will have a reasonably foreseeable material
financial effect on Lahr Motors because he is the president of
the company and because it is a source of income to him of $250
or more. (See Sections 87100 and 87103(c) and (d).)

Stanley M. Roden
November 22, 1982
Page 5


is a source of income of at leat $250 to Lahr. Many clients who
paid Lahr Motors $291 or more are oil companies who are actively
developing and producing oil and gas in Santa Barbara County.
If it is reasonably foreseeable that any of these companies will
be materially affected by the proposed zoning and permit
changes, then Commissioner Lahr may not make or participate in
the future, final decision to alter the oil and gas regulations,
nor should he have participated in the past discussions or
decisions. A company must have fairly limited activities in the
area not to be materially affected by the potential wider-spread
impact of the proposed zoning and permit changes. However, the
guidelines established in 2 Cal. Adm. Code Section
18702(b)(3)(C),5/ must be applied to the facts of each case.

COMMISSIONER STILLMAN

Commissioner Stillman owns a little over 1 percent of the
stock issued by Lompoc Oil Developing Company, worth over
$1,000, and he sits on the company's Board of Directors. Lompoc
Oil owns the mineral rights to approximately 750 acres in Santa
Barbara County. The mineral rights on 550 of the acres are
leased to Santa Fe Energy Company, which has not begun drilling
on the land. Lompoc Oil retained the mineral rights to the
remaining 200 acres, but has not yet developed them.

Because Commissioner Stillman has an ownership interest in
Lompoc Oil, valued at over $1,000, and, because he is a director
of the company, he may not vote to approve or reject the
proposed zoning and permit changes, if it is reasonably
foreseeable that the proposed changes would have a material
financial effect on Lompoc Oil. (Sections 8700 and 8710(a)
and (d).) A decision will have a material financial effect on a
company the size of Lompoc Oil (which had $7,250 in gross income
and $5,000 in net income for 1981), if the decision will affect
the company by $1,000 or more. (2 Cal. Adm. Code Section
18700(b)(1)(A)-(C).)

The facts, as described to me, do not establish that it is
reasonably foreseeable that the proposed changes will have a

5/ Union Oil Company was a client of Lahr Motors
(although it is unclear how much Union Oil paid the company
between November 1981 and November 1982) and it does extensive
work in Santa Barbara County. If the amount paid during the 12
month period was $291, or more, then Commissioner Lahr must
disqualify himself.

Stanley M. Roden
November 22, 1982
Page 6


material financial effect on Lompoc Oil. (See my letter for the
analysis.) Thus, Commissioner Stillman does not have a conflict
and he may make and participate in future decisions concerning
the changes to the oil and gas land use regulations, and it was
proper for him to participate in any discussions and decision
that may have already occurred.

CONCLUSION

As I previously indicated, my intention in writing you is to
apprise you of the facts of this situation, so that your office
may act in whatever manner it sees fit. If you decide to
consider this matter further, I recommend that you speak with
Jack Cohan, in the County Counsel's Office for updated
information on the Planning Commission's activities concerning
the oil and gas zoning amendments. If you have any additional
questions, please feel free to contact me at (916) 322-5901.

Very truly yours,

[signature]
Janis Shank McLean
Counsel
Legal Division

cc: Robert Blasier

KENNETH L. NELSON                    COUNTY COUNSEL                    105 East Anapamu Street
County Counsel                         SANTA BARBARA COUNTY                Santa Barbara, CA 93101-2063
MARVIN LEVINE                              November 22nd, 1982                Telephone: (805) 963-7189
Chief Assistant


Honorable Board of Supervisors
County of Santa Barbara
Santa Barbara, CA

Gentlemen:

                    Re:  Conflict of Interest Opinion No. A-82-155
                         (Hamister, Lahr, Stillman)

     In response to the concerns expressed by your Board, I
have the following information to report.

     Attorney General's Advice

     I spoke with Deputy Attorney General Jeffrey J. Fuller
of the State Government Section.  Mr. Fuller gave the following
advice.

     1.  A request by your Board for a formal A.G.
opinion would, if consented to by the A.G.,
require approximately 90 days for a response.
However, it is not certain the A.G. would con-
sent to review an opinion on this subject
matter since it could be considered as forum
shopping, and especially since state law has
granted the FPPC a paramount position in
matters concerning the Political Reform Act of
1974.

     Mr. Fuller further advised he would re-
commend to his superiors against issuing such
an opinion.

     2.  If the Attorney General did review the FPPC
opinion and reached a contrary conclusion, it is
unlikely it would be given as great weight as
the FPPC opinion.  This is because State law
has delegated to the FPPC the "primary responsi-
bility for the impartial, effective administra-
tion and implementation of this (Act)."
(Government Code § 83111.)


                                                     /cont'd...

John M. Cohan

is reasonably foreseeable that the company will be materially

Also, Commissioner Lahr must

I would like to point out that the provisions of the 
Political Reform Act only allow this office to render advice as 
to whether a future action of a public official will constitute 
a violation of the Act. 
Consequently, I cannot provide you with

the Act, in the past, by making or participating in the making

You asked about the effect of the Commission's past

decisions.

decisions may be set aside in a lawsuit brought by a person 
residing in the jurisdiction.

sections.facts:
The facts, as I understand them, are that Santa Barbara 
County is currently reviewing and revising its zoning 
ordinance. 
This review includes that portion of the zoning 
ordinance regulating land use for oil and gas facilities 
(Divisions 8 and 10). 
The County's Resource Management 
Department began the review process by preparing a draft of 
Divisons 8 and 10, containing the staff's recommendations. 
This 
draft was presented to the Planning Commission and hearings were 
conducted. 
As a result of these hearings, and after discussions 
by the Planning Commission, a final Revised Hearing Draft of 
Divisions 8 and 10 (dated 8/19/82) was prepared, containing 
textual changes requested by the Commission. 
The Planning Commission will vote on whether or not to 
approve the Revised Hearing Draft. 
If passed, the Draft will be 
submitted to the Board of Supervisors where it may be adopted, 
modified or disapproved. 
(See Government Code Sections 
65854-65857)!/ 
However, the recommendations of the Planning 
!/ Hereinafter John M. Cohan 
November 5, 1982  
Commission will greatly influence the Board of Supervisors in 
its final decision regarding the oil and gas regulations. 
The changes proposed by the Planning Commission would alter 
the existing oil and gas zoning provisions and permit 
procedures. 
These changes include: 
1. 
Banning oil development in seven areas where 
development was previously allowed; 
2. 
Streamlining the permit procedure for oil and gas 
exploration and production drilling by eliminating a 
requirement that oil companies obtain both a rezoning and a 
permit, and requiring, instead, that only a Conditional Use 
Permit be obtained; 
3. 
Establishing more stringent "development 
standards"~1 that must be met for exploratory oil and gas 
drilling; onshore oil and gas production; treatment and 
processing facilities; refining; marine terminals; and oil 
and gas pipelines; 
4. 
Establishing a procedure whereby a ministerially 
granted exploration and production permit may be obtained if 
a project meets certain, established criteria,ll but if 
the criteria are not met, a discretionary permit must be 
sought from the Planning Commission. 
This new procedure 
provides the Planning Commission with more discretion'than 
under the existing system.il 
~I For example, in the Revised Hearing Draft, the 
"development standards" for treatment and processing facilities 
require noise and noxious odor limitations, a fence surrounding 
the facility and provisions to prevent erosion and flood damage. 
11 
These criteria establish that the project will not 
have a significant environmental impact. 
il Under the existing system, a dicretionary, rather 
than ministerial, permit must be obtained if drilling will be 
within 1,000 feet of a zone on which drilling is not allowed. 
The new system requires a discretionary permit if drilling is 
within this 1,000 foot setback, as well as in many other 
situations, such as where the company plans to build a treatment 
or processing facility. 


John M. Cohan 
November 5, 1982  
5. 
Allowing oil companies additional flexibility in 
obtaining permits for exploratory wells. 
Currently, one 
permit is issued for both the exploration and production 
stages of oil and gas development. 
In the case of 
unproductive wells,21 this results in unnecessary, lengthy 
environmental review of the production phase. 
The proposed 
procedures allow oil companies to first file for an 
exploration permit and later file for a production permit, 
if the well is successful. 
6. 
Replacing existing, vague provisions for oil and 
gas pipelines with provisions requiring the Planning 
Commission to issue discretionary permits. 
"Development 
standards" are also established for pipelines (see #3, 
above) • 
Your question is whether Commissioners Hamister, Lahr and 
Stillman may vote on the Revised Hearing Draft, or whether they 
have conflicts of interests requiring them to abstain. 
PLANNING COMMISSIONER HAMISTER 
Planning Commissioner Hamister currently owns common stock 
in Exxon Corporation, valued at between $10,001 and $100,000. 
Section 87100 provides that no local government official shall 
make, or participate in the making of, a governmental decision 
in which she knows, or has reason to know, she has a financial 
interest. 
An official has a financial interest in a decision 
within the meaning of Section 87100 if it is reasonably 
foreseeable that the decision will have a material financial 
effect on any business entity in which the public official has a 
direct or indirect investment worth more than $1,000. 
(Section 
87103(a) ). 
Thus, Commissioner Hamister must abstain from making 
and participating in any decisions which will materially affect 
Exxon Corporation. 
In voting to approve or reject the final draft of the 
proposed changes to the zoning ordinance, Commissioner Hamister 
will be making and participating in the making of a governmental 
21 
Most of the exploratory drilling results in 
unproductive wells. 


John M. Cohan 
November 5, 1982  
decision.~1 It is true that the Board of Supervisors makes 
the final decision to change the oil and gas regulations, 
however, the law specifies that the Planning Commission has a 
major role in any zoning ordinance change. 
The Commission is 
required to hold hearings and to make a written recommendation 
(the Final Revised Hearing Draft) to the Board of Supervisors 
and, if the Board deviates from the recommendations, the Board 
must ask for an additional report and recommendation from the 
Planning Commission (Sections 65854-65857). 
Thus, the content 
of the Final Revised Hearing Draft will substantially influence 
the final changes that will be made to the oil and gas 
regulations. 
A decision to approve or reject the draft of the proposed 
zoning changes will have a material financial effect on a 
company the size of Exxon Corporation, if it is reasonably 
foreseeable that the proposed changes would increase or decrease 
Exxon's annualized gross income by $100,000, its annual net 
income by $50,000, or the value of its assets or liabilities by 
$100,000. 
(2 Cal. Adm. Code Section 18702(b) (1) (A)-(C) .)21 
In determining whether such a financial effect i~ foreseeable, 
certainty is not required and there must only be a "substantial 
likelihood" 
that the effect will occur. 
(Thorner Opinion, No. 
75-089, 1 FPPC Opinions 198, copy enclosed.) 
It is reasonably foreseeable that the proposed changes will 
have a material financial effect on Exxon Corporation, if the 
~I 
2 Cal. Adm. Code Section 18700(b) (1) states that a 
public official "makes a governmental decision" when, acting 
within the authority of his or her office, he or she votes on a 
matter. 
2 Cal. Adm. Code Section 18700 (c) (2) (B) provides that a 
public employee "participates in the making of a governmental 
decision when, acting within the authority of her position, he 
or she advises or makes recommendations to the decision-maker, 
either directly or without significant intervening substantive 
review, by preparing or presenting any report, analysis or 
opinion, orally or in writing, which requires the exercise of 
judgment on the part of the official and the purpose of which is 
to influence the decision. 
21 
2 Cal. Adm. Code Section 18702 (b) (1) (A) - (C) provides 
that, in determining whether a decision's effect on a business 
entity will be "material," consideration should be given to 
whether the effect of the decision will be to increase or 
decrease: 


John M. Cohan 
November 5, 1982  
company plans to continue the exploration, drilling and 
construction of oil and gas facilities in the area. 
The 
proposed changes will, among other things, affect the company by 
restricting the amount of developable land, altering the permit 
procedure, and subjecting its permit requests to additional 
discretionary review. 
Thus, Commissioner Hamister is required 
to disqualify herself from voting to approve or reject the Final 
Revised Hearing Draft. 
PLANNING COMMISSIONER LAHR 
Planning Commissioner Lahr is the president and major 
shareholder (86%)~1 of Lahr Electric Motors, which repairs and 
sells electric motors to oil companies.21 
His ownership 
interest is worth more than $100,000 101 and thus, he is 
prohibited from making and participating in any decision, which

or

(Section 82034.) 
21 
Among other clients. 
~I This was disclosed on his 1981 Statement of Economic 
Interests. 


John M. Cohan 
November 5, 1982  
will have a reasonably foreseeable material financial effect on 
the company. 
(Sections 87100 and 87103(a) .)11/ 
A decision will have a material financial effect on a 
company the size of Lahr Motors,12/ if it is reasonably 
foreseeable that the decision will increase or decrease the 
company's annualized gross revenues by 1 percent, its net income 
by 1/2 percent, or the value of its assets or liabilities by 
1/2 percent. 
The gross income of Lahr Motors, in 1981, was 
$600,000. 
Thus, if it is reasonably foreseeable that the 
proposed zoning and permit changes, if adopted, will increase or 
decrease the company's gross revenues by $6,000, or meet any of 
the other percentage tests, then Commissioner Lahr must 
disqualify himself. 
It is reasonably foreseeable that the proposed zoning 
changes will restrict oil and gas drilling and, therefore, cause 
a potential decline in the company's motor sales. 
However, Lahr 
Motors sells motors to businesses other than oil companies and 
it performs repairs, neither of which will be affected by the 
proposed zoning changes. 
Commissioner Lahr must, in good faith, 
determine whether it is reasonably foreseeable that the proposed 
changes, if implemented, will affect his business to the extent 
described in the percentage tests. 
He must disqualify himself 
from the vote, if necessary. 
Because Commissioner Lahr owns 86.1% of Lahr Motors, income 
to the company is treated as income to Lahr on a pro rata basis 
(Section 82030). 
Therefore, Commissioner Lahr is also 
prohibited from making and participating in a decision which 
will have a reasonably foreseeable material financial effect on 
any clients of Lahr Motors who provided the company with 
sufficient gross income, during the 12 months prior to the vote 
on the Revised Hearing Draft,ll/ that Commission Lahr's pro 
rata share of the income (86%) was $250 or more. 
(To meet this 
11/ 
He is also prohibited from making and participating 
in decisions that will have a reasonably foreseeable material 
financial effect on Lahr Motors because he is the president of 
the company and because it is a source of income to him of $250 
or more. 
(See Sections 87100 and 87103(c) and (d).) 
12/ 
The test for a "material" financial effect varies 
with the amount of a company's annualized gross income, net 
income, and assets and liabilities. 
13/ 
Assuming that the Planning Commission will vote on 
the changes in approximately November 1982, the period covered 
begins in November 1981. 


John M. Cohan 
November 5, 1982  
threshold a client only needed to pay $291 to Lahr Motors.) Many 
of the clients who paid Lahr Motors $291 or more are oil 
companies who are actively developing and producing oil and gas 
in Santa Barbara County. 
If it is reasonably foreseeable that 
any of these companies will be materially affected by the 
proposed zoning and permit changes, then Commissioner Lahr must 
disqualify himself from making or participating in the vote. 
A 
company must have fairly limited activities in the area not to 
be materially affected by the potential wide-spread impact of 
the proposed zoning and permit changes. 
However, the facts of 
each case must be applied to the guidelines established in 
2 Cal. Adm. Code Section 18702(b) (3) (C) .14/ 
COMMISSIONER STILLMAN 
Commissioner Stillman owns a little over 1 percent of the 
stock issued by Lompoc Oil Developing Company, worth over 
$1,000, and he sits on the company's Board of Directors. 
Lompoc 
Oil owns the mineral rights to approximately 750 acres in Santa 
Barbara County.15/ 
The mineral rights on 550 of the acres are 
leased to Santa Fe Energy Company, which has not begun drilling 
on the land. 16/ 
Lompoc Oil retained the mineral rights to the 
remaining 200 acres, but has not yet developed them. 
Because Commissioner Stillman has an ownership interest in 
Lompoc Oil, valued at over $1,000, and, because he is a director 
of the company, he may not vote to approve or reject the 
14/ 
See the discussion regarding Exxon Corporation 
contained in this letter. 
Union Oil Company was a client of Lahr Motors (although it 
is unclear how much Union Oil paid the company between November 
1981 and November 1982) and it does extensive work in Santa 
Barbara County. 
If the amount paid during the 12 month period 
was $291, or more, then Commissioner Lahr must disqualify 
himself. 
15/ 
The facts regarding Lompoc Oil are drawn from your 
letters; my conversations with your office; a letter from 
David A. Anderson, attorney for Get Oil Out, Inc., and GOO Legal 
and Educational Fund, Inc.; and an article from the Santa 
Barbara News-Press dated May 27, 1982. 
16/ If any future drilling by Santa Fe Energy Company is 
successful, Lompoc Oil will receive a portion of the profit. 


John M. Cohan 
November 5, 1982  
proposed zoning and permit changes, if the changes, if adopted, 
will have a reasonably foreseeable material financial effect on 
Lompoc Oil. 
(Sections 87100 and 87103(a) and (d).) 
A decision 
will have a material financial effect on a company the size of 
Lompoc Oil (which had $7,250 in gross income and $5,000 in net 
income for 1981), if the decision will affect the company by 
$1,000 or more. 
(2 Cal. Adm. Code Section 18700(b) (1) (A)-(C).) 
The mineral rights owned by Lompoc Oil are on land zoned 
Agricultural - 100 (meaning that there is a 100 acre minimum) . 
Under the exisiting zoning ordinance, drilling is allowed on 
this type of land as a matter of right, unless the drilling is 
within 1,000 feet of another zone that does not allow drilling. 
In the latter case, a Conditional Use Permit must be obtained, 
which involves a discretionary review by the Planning 
Commmission. 
Under the proposed zoning changes, the 700 acres 
will probably be designated as Agricultural II land. 
On 
Agricultural II land, drilling is allowed as a matter of right, 
if the land meets certain criteria. 
If the criteria are not 
met, an Exploratory Plan must be prepared and the Planning 
Commission's discretionary approval must be obtained. 
Based on these facts, it is not reasonably foreseeable that 
the proposed changes will have a material financial effect on 
Lompoc Oil because there will be little or no impact on the 
company's gross income, net income, or assets. 
The changes may, 
ultimately, cost Lompoc Oil some additonal money in permit 
costs, however, the company currently has no plans to develop 
the 200 acres and the remaining 550 acres will be developed by 
Santa Fe Energy Company. 
Also, any increase in the Planning 
Commission's discretion in granting or denying permits is 
unlikely to affect the company because, assuming that a 
discretionary permit is necessary at all, the zoning of the land 
makes the approval of a permit highly probable. 17/ 
Thus, 
Commissioner Stillman may vote on the proposed changes, as he 
does not appear to have a conflict.
sections.analysis:
. 
The Political Reform Act prevents public officials 
from making, or participating in the making of, governmental 
decisions (Section 87100). 
An official "participates in the 
making of a governmental decision" when, acting within the 
authority of his or her position, he or she: 
(2) 
Advises or makes recommendations to the 
decision-maker, either directly or without significant 
intervening substantive review, by: 
(A) 
Conducting research or making any 
investigation which requires the exercise of 
judgment on the part of the official ••• and the 
purpose of which is to influence the decision; or 
(B) 
Preparing or presenting any report, 
analysis or opinion, orally or in writing, which 
requires the exercise of judgment on the part of 
the official and the purpose of which is to 
influence the decision. 
(2 Cal. Adm. Code Section 
18700 (c) (2) (A) and (B).) 
The following discussion considers the application of the 
provisions of the Political Reform Act to the facts surrounding 
the financial interests of each of the Commissioners and 
indicates where violations of the Act may nave occurred. 
1:./ 
Hereinafter Stanley M. Roden 
November 22, 1982  
PLANNING COMMISSIONER HAMISTER 
Planning Commissioner Hamister currently owns common stock 
in Exxon Corporation, valued at between $10,001 and $100,000. 
Section 87100 provides that no local government official shall 
make, or participate in the making of, a governmental decision 
in which she knows, or has reason to know, she has a financial 
interest. 
An official has a financial interest in a decision 
within the meaning of Section 87100 if it is reasonably 
foreseeable that the decision will have a material financial 
effect on any business entity in which the public official has a 
direct or indirect investment worth more than $1,000. 
(Section 
87103(a)). 
Thus, Commissioner Hamister must abstain from making 
and participating in any decisions which will materially affect 
Exxon Corporation. 
Under Commission regulations, a decision 
will have a material financial effect on a company the size of 
Exxon, if it is reasonably foreseeable that the effect of the 
decision will be to increase or decrease Exxon's annualized 
gross income by $100,000, its annual net income by $50,000, or 
the value of its assets or liabilities by $100,000. 
(2 Cal. 
Adm. Code Section 18702(b) (1) (A)-(C).) 
As I discussed in my advice letter to Mr. Cohan, it is 
reasonably foreseeable that the decision to amend the oil and 
gas regulations is a decision will have a material financial 
effect on Exxon Corporation. 
(See my letter for the analysis.) 
Thus, Commissioner Hamister is and was prohibited from making, 
or participating in the making of, this decision. 
In my advice letter, I confined my analysis to whether it 
was proper for Commissioner Hamister to vote on the future, 
final decision to approve or reject the Final Revised Draft of 
the changes to the oil and gas regulations. 
However, 
Commissioner Hamister may have already participated in 
discussions or decisions occurring prior to this final vote. 
In 
my letter, I did not discuss the propriety of her participation 
and any enforcement action would, of course, focus on these past 
activities. 
PLANNING COMMISSIONER LAHR 
Planning Commissioner Lahr is the president and major 
shareholder (86%)~/ of Lahr Electric Motors, which repairs and 
~/ For purposes of the Political Reform Act, an 
official's ownership interest in a business entity includes any 
interest owned directly, indirectly or beneficially by his or 
her immediate family. 
(Section 82034.) 


Stanley M. Roden 
November 22, 1982  
sells electric motors to oil companies.i/ 
His ownership 
interest is worth more than $lOO,oooi/ and thus, he is 
prohibited from maKing and participating in any decision, in 
which it is reasonably foreseeable that it will have a material 
financial effect on the company. 
(Sections 87100 and 
87103 (a) . )~/ 
A decision has a material financial effect on a company the 
size of Lahr Motors, if it increases or decreases the company's 
annualized gross revenues by 1 percent, its net income by 
1/2 percent, or the value of its assets or liabilities by 
1/2 percent. 
The gross income of Lahr Motors, in 1981, was 
$600,000. 
Thus, if it is reasonably foreseeable that the 
proposed zoning and permit changes, if adopted, will increase or 
decrease the company's gross revenues by $6,000, or meet any of 
the other percentage tests, then Commissioner Lahr must 
disqualify himself from the future final vote and he should have 
abstained from any past participation in this decision. 
It is reasonably foreseeable that the proposed zoning 
changes will restrict oil and gas drilling. 
This change has the 
potential to cause a decline in the company's motor sales. 
However, Lahr Motors sells motors to and performs repairs-for 
other businesses than oil companies. 
Thus, much of its business 
will not be affected by the proposed zoning changes. 
In my 
letter, I advised Commissioner Lahr that he must, in good faith, 
determine whether it is reasonably foreseeable that the proposed 
changes, if implemented, will affect his business to the extent 
described in the percentage tests. 
Because Commissioner Lahr owns 86% of Lahr Motors, a 
EE£ rata (86%) share of the income to the company is treated as 
income to Lahr (Section 82030). 
Therefore, any client who paid 
Lahr Motors at least $291 or more in the past 12 months 
1/ 
Among other clients. 
!/ This was disclosed on his 1981 Statement of Economic 
Interests. 
~/ He is also prohibited from making and participating in 
decisions that will have a reasonably foreseeable material 
financial effect on Lahr Motors because he is the president of 
the company and because it is a source of income to him of $250 
or more. 
(See Sections 87100 and 87103(c) and (d).) 


Stanley M. Roden 
November 22, 1982  
is a source of income of at leat $250 to Lahr. 
Many clients who 
paid Lahr Motors $291 or more are oil companies who are actively 
developing and producing oil and gas in Santa Barbara County. 
If it is reasonably foreseeable that any of these companies will 
be materially affected by the proposed zoning and permit 
changes, then Commissioner Lahr may not make or participate in 
the future, final decision to alter the oil and gas regulations, 
nor should he have particpated in the past discussions or 
decisions. 
A company must have fairly limited activities in the 
area not to be materially affected by the potential wide-spread 
impact of the proposed zoning and permit changes. 
However, the 
guidelines established in 2 Cal. Adm. Code Section 
18702(b) (3) (C) ,2/ must be applied to the facts of each case. 
COMMISSIONER STILLMAN 
Commissioner Stillman owns a little over 1 percent of the 
stock issued by Lompoc Oil Developing Company, worth over 
$1,000, and he sits on the company's Board of Directors. 
Lompoc 
Oil owns the mineral rights to approximately 750 acres in Santa 
Barbara County. 
The mineral rights on 550 of the acres are 
leased to Santa Fe Energy Company, which has not begun drilling 
on the land. 
Lompoc Oil retained the mineral rights to the 
remaining 200 acres, but has not yet developed them. 
Because Commissioner Stillman has an ownership interest in 
Lompoc Oil, valued at over $1,000, and, because he is a director 
of the company, he may not vote to approve or reject the 
proposed zoning and permit changes, if it is reasonably 
foreseeable that the proposed changes would have a material 
financial effect on Lompoc Oil. 
(Sections 87100 and 87103(a) 
and (d).) 
A decision will have a material financial effect on a 
company the size of Lompoc Oil (which had $7,250 in gross income 
and $5,000 in net income for 1981), if the decision will affect 
the company by $1,000 or more. 
(2 Cal. Adm. Code Section 
18700 (b) (1) (A) - (C) .) 
The facts, as described to me, do not establish that it is 
reasonably foreseeable that the proposed changes will have a 
~/ Union Oil Company was a client of Lahr Motors 
(although it is unclear how much Union Oil paid the company 
between November 1981 and November 1982) and it does extensive 
work in Santa Barbara County. 
If the amount paid during the 12 
month period was $291, or more, then Commissioner Lahr must 
disqualify himself. 


Stanley M. Roden 
November 22, 1982  
material financial effect on Lompoc Oil. 
(See my letter for the 
analysis.) 
Thus, Commissioner Stillman does not have a conflict 
and he may make and participate in future decisions concerning 
the changes to the oil and gas land use regulations, and it was 
proper for him to participate in any discussions and decision 
that may have already occurred. 
CONCLUSION 
As I previously indicated, my intention in writing you is to 
apprise you of the facts of this situation, so that your office 
may act in whatever manner it sees fit. 
If you decide to 
consider this matter further, I recommend that you speak with 
Jack Cohan, in the County Counsel's Office for updated 
information on the Planning Commission's activities concerning 
the oil and gas zoning amendments. 
If you have any additional

CONCLUSION: If Exxon Corporation plans to continue to explore, drill and 
develop oil and gas facilities in Santa Barbara County, then it

Also, the land surrounding this property has

John M. Cohan 
November 5, 1982  
is reasonably foreseeable that the company will be materially 
affected by the proposed changes and Commissioner Hamister must 
disqualify herself from voting. 
Also, Commissioner Lahr must 
make a good faith determination as to whether he must disqualify 
himself from the vote. 
It appears likely that the proposed 
changes, if adopted, will either materially affect clients of 
his business who are sources of income to him, or the business 
itself. 
In either case, disqualification is necessary. 
Lastly, 
Commissioner Stillman does not have a conflict of interest and 
may vote on the proposed changes. 
I would like to point out that the provisions of the 
Political Reform Act only allow this office to render advice as 
to whether a future action of a public official will constitute 
a violation of the Act. 
Consequently, I cannot provide you with 
advice on whether Commissioners Hamister and Lahr 181 violated 
the Act, in the past, by making or participating in the making 
of decisions involving changes to the oil and gas regulations. 
In addition, the staff's advice provides the Commissioners with 
immunity from enforcement actionsl11 only if the advice is 
requested prior to the violationlQl (2 Cal. Adm. Code Section 
18329) • 
You asked about the effect of the Commission's past 
activities on the decisions which have already been made. 
Even 
if the past activities were in violation of the Act, the 
provisions of the Political Reform Act do not invalidate these 
decisions. 
However, under special circumstances, these 
decisions may be set aside in a lawsuit brought by a person 
residing in the jurisdiction. 
(Section 91003(b}.)

John M. Cohan

I hope my letter is helpful to you. 
If you have any 
additional questions, please feel free to contact me at

embedding.first_500_words: State of California Fair Political Practices Commission P.O. BOX 807 • SACRAMENTO, 95804 • • • 1100 K STREET BUILDING, SACRAMENTO, 95814 Technical Assistance • • Administration • • Executive/Legal • • Enforcement • • Statements of Economic Intere (916) 322-5662 322-5660 322-5901 322-6441 322-6444 November 5, 1982 John M. Cohan Deputy County Counsel 105 East Anapamu St. Santa Barbara, CA 93101-2063 Re: Advice Request No. A-82-155 Dear Mr. Cohan: Thank you for your request for advice on behalf of Santa Barbara County Planning Commissioners Margaret S. Hamister, Donald E. Lahr and Kenneth R. Stillman. FACTS The facts, as I understand them, are that Santa Barbara County is currently reviewing and revising its zoning ordinance. This review includes that portion of the zoning ordinance regulating land use for oil and gas facilities (Divisions 8 and 10). The County's Resource Management Department began the review process by preparing a draft of Divisions 8 and 10, containing the staff's recommendations. This draft was presented to the Planning Commission and hearings were conducted. As a result of these hearings, and after discussions by the Planning Commission, a final Revised Hearing Draft of Divisions 8 and 10 (dated 8/19/82) was prepared, containing textual changes requested by the Commission. The Planning Commission will vote on whether or not to approve the Revised Hearing Draft. If passed, the Draft will be submitted to the Board of Supervisors where it may be adopted, modified or disapproved. (See Government Code Sections 65854-65857)1/ However, the recommendations of the Planning ___________________ 1/ Hereinafter all statutory references are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. John M. Cohan November 5, 1982 Page 2 Commission will greatly influence the Board of Supervisors in its final decision regarding the oil and gas regulations. The changes proposed by the Planning Commission would alter the existing oil and gas zoning provisions and permit procedures. These changes include: 1. Banning oil development in seven areas where development was previously allowed; 2. Streamlining the permit procedure for oil and gas exploration and production drilling by eliminating a requirement that oil companies obtain both a rezoning and a permit, and requiring, instead, that only a Conditional Use Permit be obtained; 3. Establishing more stringent "development standards"2/ that must be met for exploratory oil and gas drilling; onshore oil and gas production; treatment and processing facilities; refining; marine terminals; and oil and gas pipelines; 4. Establishing a procedure whereby a ministerially granted exploration and production permit may be obtained if a project meets certain, established criteria.1/ But if the criteria are not met, a discretionary permit must be sought from the Planning Commission. This new procedure provides the Planning Commission with more discretion than under the existing system.4/ _______________________ 2/ For example, in the Revised Hearing Draft, the "development standards" for treatment and processing facilities require noise and noxious odor limitations, a fence surrounding the facility and provisions to prevent erosion and flood damage. 3/ These criteria establish that the project will not have a significant environmental impact. 4/
